source: CIS GOOGLE KUBERNETES ENGINE (GKE) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF
title: Consider firewalling GKE worker nodes

input_row.source: CIS GOOGLE KUBERNETES ENGINE (GKE) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF
input_row.title: Consider firewalling GKE worker nodes
input_row.description: Reduce the network attack surface of GKE nodes by using Firewalls to restrict ingress and egress traffic.
input_row.rationale: Utilizing stringent ingress and egress firewall rules minimizes the ports and services exposed to an network-based attacker, whilst also restricting egress routes within or out of the cluster in the event that a compromised component attempts to form an outbound connection. Impact: All instances targeted by a firewall rule, either using a tag or a service account will be affected. Ensure there are no adverse effects on other instances using the target tag or service account before implementing the firewall rule.
input_row.audit: Using Google Cloud Console: 1. Go to Compute Engine by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/compute/instances. 2. For each instance within your cluster, use the 'more actions' menu (3 vertical dots) and select to 'View network details'. 3. If there are multiple network interfaces attached to the instance, select the network interface to view in the 'Network interface' details section and see all the rules that apply to the network interface, within the 'Firewall rules' tab. Make sure the firewall rules are appropriate for your environment. Using Command Line: For the instance being evaluated, obtain its Service account and tags: gcloud compute instances describe <instance_name> --zone <compute_zone> -- format json | jq '{tags: .tags.items[], serviceaccount:.serviceAccounts[].email, network: .networkInterfaces[].network}' This will return: { "tags": "<tag>", "serviceaccount": "<service_account>" "network": "https://www.googleapis.com/compute/v1/projects/<project_id>/global/networks/ <network>" } Then, observe the firewall rules applied to the instance by using the following command, replacing <tag> and <service_account> as appropriate: gcloud compute firewall-rules list \ --format="table( name, network, direction, priority, sourceRanges.list():label=SRC_RANGES, destinationRanges.list():label=DEST_RANGES, allowed[].map().firewall_rule().list():label=ALLOW, denied[].map().firewall_rule().list():label=DENY, sourceTags.list():label=SRC_TAGS, sourceServiceAccounts.list():label=SRC_SVC_ACCT, targetTags.list():label=TARGET_TAGS, targetServiceAccounts.list():label=TARGET_SVC_ACCT, disabled )" \ --filter="targetTags.list():<tag> OR targetServiceAccounts.list():<service_account>" Firewall rules may also be applied to a network without specifically targeting Tags or Service Accounts. These can be observed using the following, replacing <network> as appropriate: gcloud compute firewall-rules list \ --format="table( name, network, direction, priority, sourceRanges.list():label=SRC_RANGES, destinationRanges.list():label=DEST_RANGES, allowed[].map().firewall_rule().list():label=ALLOW, denied[].map().firewall_rule().list():label=DENY, sourceTags.list():label=SRC_TAGS, sourceServiceAccounts.list():label=SRC_SVC_ACCT, targetTags.list():label=TARGET_TAGS, targetServiceAccounts.list():label=TARGET_SVC_ACCT, disabled )" \ --filter="network.list():<network> AND -targetTags.list():* AND - targetServiceAccounts.list():*"
input_row.remediation: Using Google Cloud Console: 1. Go to Firewall Rules by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/networking/firewalls/list 2. Click CREATE FIREWALL RULE. 3. Configure the firewall rule as required. Ensure the firewall targets the nodes correctly, either selecting the nodes using tags (under Targets, select Specified target tags, and set Target tags to <tag>), or using the Service account associated with node (under Targets, select Specified service account, set Service account scope as appropriate, and Target service account to <service_account>). 4. Click CREATE. Using Command Line: Use the following command to generate firewall rules, setting the variables as appropriate: gcloud compute firewall-rules create <firewall_rule_name> --network <network> --priority <priority> --direction <direction> --action <action> --target-tags <tag> --target-service-accounts <service_account> --source-ranges <source_cidr_range> --source-tags <source_tags> --source-service-accounts <source_service_account> --destination-ranges <destination_cidr_range> -- rules <rules> Default Value: Every VPC network has two implied firewall rules. These rules exist, but are not shown in the Cloud Console: • The implied allow egress rule: An egress rule whose action is allow, destination is 0.0.0.0/0, and priority is the lowest possible (65535) lets any instance send traffic to any destination, except for traffic blocked by GCP. Outbound access may be restricted by a higher priority firewall rule. Internet access is allowed if no other firewall rules deny outbound traffic and if the instance has an external IP address or uses a NAT instance. • The implied deny ingress rule: An ingress rule whose action is deny, source is 0.0.0.0/0, and priority is the lowest possible (65535) protects all instances by blocking incoming traffic to them. Incoming access may be allowed by a higher priority rule. Note that the default network includes some additional rules that override this one, allowing certain types of incoming traffic. The implied rules cannot be removed, but they have the lowest possible priorities. References: 1. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/cluster-architecture 2. https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/using-firewalls
input_row.impact: All instances targeted by a firewall rule, either using a tag or a service account will be affected. Ensure there are no adverse effects on other instances using the target tag or service account before implementing the firewall rule.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The control around firewalling GKE worker nodes in GCP can be verified using GCP APIs and Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) commands. Firewall rules in GCP are standard configurations available through the GCP API, which means they can be programmatically listed and evaluated. The audit instructions provide precise gcloud commands to list and evaluate existing firewall rules applicable to GKE worker nodes, indicating that the verification can be automated.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_compute_instance_firewall_rules_verified

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **GCP API Calls/Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) Methods:** Use gcloud to list firewall rules applied to GKE worker nodes. Specifically, utilize the `gcloud compute firewall-rules list` command to retrieve detailed rules descriptions.
- **GCP CLI Commands:** Execute `gcloud compute instances describe <instance_name> --zone <compute_zone> --format json` to obtain tags and service accounts for each instance. Then, use filtered `gcloud compute firewall-rules list` commands to inspect the applicable firewall rules.
- **Specific Resources to Enumerate:** Enumerate GKE worker nodes by querying Compute Engine instances that belong to Kubernetes clusters in the specified project and zone.
- **Configuration Properties/Settings to Validate:** Validate the configuration of firewall rules for GKE nodes to ensure they are appropriately restricted. Essential properties include checking the allowed and denied ports and IPs, ensuring ingress and egress restrictions meet security requirements.
- **Clear Pass/Fail Criteria with Expected Values:** 
  - **Pass:** All GKE nodes have firewall rules that restrict unnecessary ingress and egress traffic, aligning with organizational security requirements.
  - **Fail:** GKE nodes have open firewall rules that allow unnecessary or excessive ingress/egress that might increase exposure to network attacks.

Evaluating these configurations programmatically allows for scalable and consistent compliance checking across multiple GKE worker nodes and clusters within the GCP environment.
